lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that control and operate stand-alone wood-, wood-waste-, or agricultural-residue-fueled electric power plants and repeatedly sell electricity and associated renewable attributes to external offtakers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive industrial cogeneration; regulated-utility units; passive project or tax-equity vehicles; development-stage projects without operating generation; municipal-solid-waste incinerators whose primary economics include disposal fees; landfill-gas projects; and renewable natural gas production.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring electricity sales, typically through a power purchase agreement or wholesale market, potentially supplemented by renewable energy credits; the screened firm procures feedstock and remains accountable for plant operation, environmental compliance, and delivery.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 221117 combines stand-alone wood or residue power, captive industrial generation, landfill gas, and waste-to-energy facilities. The lens retains stand-alone solid-biomass generators selling externally because the excluded models have materially different feedstock, tipping-fee, captive-use, and control economics that would make one screen incoherent.

a.bridge: BLS measures exact-industry occupation shares, not wage-weighted task exposure, and covers biomass models excluded from the narrowed lens. Plant operators are 38.31% of employment, with management, business, administrative, engineering, maintenance, and material-handling roles comprising much of the remainder. I map monitoring, forecasting, diagnostics, documentation, scheduling, procurement analytics, and report work to exposed tasks and haircut physical fuel handling, repair, emissions testing, safety response, and existing controls; mixed biomass models can bias the result in either direction.
a.rationale: AI can assist combustion and boiler optimization, alarm triage, feedstock-quality forecasting, maintenance diagnosis, scheduling, regulatory documentation, and PPA or REC administration. Exposure is meaningful because plant operators dominate headcount, but high-temperature equipment, variable fuel, ash handling, inspection, repair, and compliance accountability keep much of the work physical or judgment-intensive.
a.change_evidence: Plant-level time-and-wage studies for the retained population, split among control-room monitoring, fuel procurement, material handling, maintenance, environmental reporting, and already-automated tasks, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: OEWS provides headcount shares rather than wage-weighted task shares.
The 2,300-worker exact-industry estimate includes landfill gas, waste biomass, and other models outside the narrowed lens.
DOE's AI use cases cover the wider power system and are not observed biomass labor savings.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible for predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, feedstock and combustion forecasting, work-order prioritization, reporting, and operator decision support. Older plants, limited instrumentation, heterogeneous fuels, small data sets, cyber and control-system constraints, emissions obligations, and thin implementation teams prevent full realization.
rho.change_evidence: A representative audit of plant sensor coverage, historian quality, control architecture, current analytics, vendor proposals, implementation cost, and verified labor-hour changes at operating biomass plants would materially move the range.
caveats: Published evidence demonstrates cross-power-sector use cases and biomass-combustion research, not broad production deployment in US LMM biomass plants.
Financially stressed or closure-risk plants may defer even positive-payback systems.

e.rationale: Among the frozen estimate of 28 LMM firms, eligibility is reduced by captive mill generation, regulated or municipal ownership, development shells, non-control project interests, landfill-gas and waste-disposal models, and plants lacking durable external contracts or viable feedstock supply. A meaningful minority should remain as independently controlled stand-alone biomass power operators, but no source enumerates them by normalized EBITDA and control status.
e.change_evidence: EIA-860 and EIA-923 plant-owner records joined to S&P Capital IQ or PitchBook financials, PPA status, captive-use share, project debt, tax-equity, and operating-control documents would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than observed and may include inactive or non-operating entities.
A 55 MW plant transaction demonstrates an eligible structure but not the population share in the target EBITDA band.

s5.bridge: Pacolet's 2024 purchase of 100% of a project company owning a 55 MW biomass plant is an observed qualifying form of control transfer, but the transaction size and target EBITDA were undisclosed and may exceed the LMM lens. I use it to establish transfer feasibility and apply a broad, low probability for any eligible firm over five years; the single visible deal may overstate recurring LMM flow or understate private transactions.
s5.rationale: Biomass project companies with long-term PPAs, established feedstock networks, and operating histories can transfer as whole equity interests. However, the US fleet is small, assets are bespoke, owners may hold plants for contract duration, and declining generation can produce closures or restructurings rather than clean control sales.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of biomass corporate and project transactions from EIA ownership changes, FERC section 203 filings, PitchBook, IJGlobal, Power Intelligence, and state utility dockets, filtered for target EBITDA and genuine control, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: One disclosed acquisition cannot establish an industry transfer rate.
The seller and buyer were institutional owners, not owner-operators with known succession needs.
No owner-age data were found for eligible private firms.

q.rationale: A fixed or long-term PPA can preserve internal operating savings before renewal, while REC sales can add a market-linked revenue stream. Retention is reduced by contract reopeners, performance and availability penalties, future PPA repricing, competitive renewable alternatives, possible regulated treatment, and the likelihood that savings must fund reliability, emissions controls, or feedstock-cost pressure.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of PPAs, REC contracts, fuel-supply agreements, and debt covenants showing price formulas, cost pass-through, availability guarantees, sharing clauses, term, and renewal dates would materially change retention.
caveats: Public announcements confirm long-term PPAs but do not disclose price, escalators, reopeners, or remaining term.
Feedstock cost and power revenue may be separately indexed, making benefit retention plant-specific.

d5.bridge: EIA measures national generation by biomass fuel across utilities, independent producers, and captive commercial or industrial plants, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed LMM external-sale service. Wood-derived generation fell from 38.543 million MWh in 2019 to 31.564 million MWh in 2024, a 0.819 ratio; I carry that decline forward with a wide range for plant retirements, contract support, fuel availability, and grid demand. Inclusion of captive and utility generation can bias the proxy relative to independent stand-alone plants in either direction.
d5.rationale: The best direct operating signal is adverse: wood-derived generation declined about 18% over the last observed five-year interval, while landfill gas and biogenic MSW also fell. Existing contracted plants may remain stable and firm power needs could support renewals, but cheaper wind, solar, storage, natural gas, feedstock competition, environmental opposition, and aging equipment constrain recovery.
d5.change_evidence: Plant-level 2026 generation, retirement filings, PPA expiry and renewal status, capacity-market awards, fuel-supply commitments, and planned conversions for every retained independent plant would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Historical generation is affected by outages, fuel prices, weather, and plant mix as well as demand.
The retained lens is narrower than EIA's fuel categories.
BLS projects only 1.9% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, but employment is not a demand measure and no exact-industry output projection is published.

o.rationale: Electricity generated by an operating solid-biomass plant still requires an accountable operator to manage feedstock acceptance, combustion, steam and turbine systems, ash, environmental permits, maintenance, grid delivery, and safety. AI can reduce labor and improve decisions but cannot replace the operating entity; the main loss is consolidation into utilities or larger fleets rather than software-only supply.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of commercially unmanned biomass plants, outsourced operator-of-record arrangements, utility insourcing, or technology vendors assuming environmental and delivery liability would materially change this share.
caveats: Operator-required demand does not imply preservation of the current independent firm count after consolidation.
The estimate concerns accountable operation, not staffing levels.

narrative.executive_view: Stand-alone biomass generators have a data-rich operating environment and a labor mix led by plant operators, creating credible opportunities in monitoring, diagnostics, maintenance planning, combustion optimization, and compliance workflow. Long-term PPAs can preserve savings and a recent 100% project-company sale confirms transferability. The counterweight is structural demand erosion: national wood-derived generation fell roughly 18% from 2019 to 2024, and the target population is obscured by captive, municipal, regulated, landfill-gas, and waste-disposal models.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can forecast feedstock quality and inventory, improve boiler and combustion settings, detect equipment anomalies, prioritize maintenance, triage alarms, and draft environmental or operating reports. These tools augment a workforce concentrated in plant operation and maintenance, but they do not remove fuel handling, ash, repair, testing, emergency response, or licensed accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Existing fixed or long-term PPAs can delay customer capture of internal cost reductions, supporting moderate retention. Savings may still be absorbed by aging-plant maintenance, emissions controls, feedstock inflation, debt restrictions, performance penalties, or lower renewal pricing when biomass competes against cheaper resources.
narrative.firm_availability: The code is economically heterogeneous, so eligibility requires direct verification of external sales, operational control, EBITDA, feedstock contracts, and PPA durability. A 2024 acquisition of all equity in a 55 MW project company shows that a contracted wood-waste plant can transfer, but does not prove frequent LMM deal flow or owner succession.
narrative.demand_durability: Every surviving plant's output remains operator-required, yet the quantity of wood-derived generation has contracted. Waste diversion, renewable attributes, firm generation needs, and contracts can support individual assets, but retirements, fuel competition, aging boilers, environmental regulation, and unfavorable power economics can continue to reduce the service basket.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are plant and PPA concentration, fuel availability and delivered cost, emissions compliance, fire and boiler outages, ash disposal, community opposition, capital needs, project debt, and a declining national generation base. The frozen firm count and compensation ratio span heterogeneous business models, and published AI evidence does not measure actual labor savings at LMM biomass plants.
narrative.principal_driver: Contracted recurring power sales and operator-heavy, instrumented thermal plants create a plausible path to retain AI-enabled operating efficiencies.
narrative.principal_weakness: A sustained decline in wood-derived generation and uncertain eligibility of the estimated LMM firms undermine five-year demand and target availability.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 221117
sources.supports: Exact-industry employment of 2,300; power plant operators 38.31%; production 48.01%; maintenance 14.85%; management 14.05%; business and financial operations 6.66%; and administrative support 4.60%.

sources.title: AI for Energy
sources.supports: DOE identifies power-sector AI functions including predictive asset maintenance, anomaly detection, state estimation from sparse measurements, outage analysis, and operational decision support.

sources.title: A review on the application of machine learning for combustion in power generation applications
sources.supports: The DOE-hosted research review covers biomass combustion and identifies modeling, prediction, forecasting, optimization, fault detection, and control as machine-learning applications in combustion power generation.

sources.title: Electric Power Annual Table 3.1.B: Net Generation from Renewable Sources
sources.supports: Wood-derived generation fell from 38.543 million MWh in 2019 to 31.564 million MWh in 2024; landfill gas fell from 10.468 million to 7.341 million MWh, while biogenic MSW fell from 6.093 million to 5.402 million MWh.

sources.title: Pacolet Milliken Acquires Piedmont Green Power
sources.supports: In January 2024 Pacolet announced acquiring 100% of a project company owning a 55 MW biomass plant that uses about 600,000 tons of wood waste annually and sells to Georgia Power under a long-term PPA.

sources.title: Small Municipal Waste Combustors: New Source Performance Standards and Emission Guidelines
sources.supports: EPA identifies Clean Air Act limits for particulate matter, carbon monoxide, dioxins and furans, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, hydrogen chloride, lead, mercury, and cadmium, illustrating operator-intensive compliance obligations in combustion-based waste power.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: BLS projects biomass electric power generation employment from 2,400 in 2024 to 2,500 in 2034, a 1.9% increase, while publishing no separate exact-industry output forecast.
